text:
Centre for Epilepsy

Neurosciences Division 

Clinic Date: 21 March 2014 

Dr Fiona  
Centre for Epilepsy  
Queen Square, London WC1N 3BG  

Dear Dr Fiona,                                                                                                         

Thomas Reed, DOB: 09-11-1984, Hospital No: H726391 NHS No. 9042175638  
Flat 3, 28 Kingfisher Road, London, SE15 4DA  

Epilepsy 

Medication: Lamotrigine titrated since early February 2014 

Current regimen: Lamotrigine dispersible 50mg in the morning and 75mg in the evening (patient-reported adherence; uses pill organiser) 

Lamotrigine Blood Level: 

10 March 2014: 3.4 mg/L (reference range 1.0 to 15.0 mg/L). Dose at time of sampling: 50mg AM / 75mg PM 

Seizures: Following two episodes of dizziness and subsequent headaches, his symptoms improved with supportive care. On 18 February the absences diminished after dose escalation. His last episode was recorded on 26 February and he has remained well since. Considering that he has now been free of seizures for three weeks and his overall condition is stable, no immediate action is necessary. 

I reviewed Thomas Reed today in clinic. He works as a warehouse picker operating between tall racking and narrow aisles, where lifting tasks and walking pace vary by shift. He reports that the earlier dizziness occurred at the end of a busy late shift and that he took breaks with oral fluids and sat out; there was no loss of awareness at work reported by colleagues. Since the lamotrigine adjustment on 18 February, he notes steadier concentration, improved sleep hygiene, and no further events since 26 February. No new neurological deficits, no morning myoclonus, and no tongue biting reported. He has not driven. 

Observations today: BP 124/76, pulse 72 regular, neurological examination unremarkable. No rash or gastrointestinal intolerance to lamotrigine. Weight stable. 

Plan: 
- Continue lamotrigine 50mg AM / 75mg PM. 
- Safety: maintain regular breaks, hydration, and prompt reporting of any recurrent episodes at work (particularly around lifting or working at height). 
- Blood level recheck if clinical change or dose adjustment needed; otherwise routine review in three months. 
- No immediate action is necessary given three weeks event-free and overall stability. Patient has contact details for rapid access if symptoms recur. 

Yours sincerely

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: Clinic date is 21 March 2014. The letter states: 'His last episode was recorded on 26 February and he has remained well since' and 'free of seizures for three weeks.' He is not seizure-free for ≥6 months. Since the dose escalation on 18 February, there has been exactly one episode (on 26 February) in the roughly one-month interval up to clinic review (18 Feb to 21 Mar ≈ 31 days). Therefore, the current frequency approximates to 1 per month.
